Authority bias.definition: The tendency to attribute greater accuracy, legitimacy, or truth to the opinion of an authority figure and to be more influenced by that opinion, even when it conflicts with one’s own judgment.
Authority bias.phishing_context: Message impersonates a figure of authority (e.g., a CEO or IT manager) to prompt compliance without questioning its legitimacy.
examples: IRS: Official inquiry regarding your recent tax return.
IT Security Team: Security alert: Urgent update required for your workstations.
HR Director request: Confirm your SSN in the attached form.

Scarcity bias.definition: The tendency to assign greater value to resources, opportunities, or objects when they are perceived as scarce or limited.
Scarcity bias.phishing_context: Message claims to have an offer or account access that is only available for a strictly limited window.
examples: Only 3 spots left to secure your year-end bonus—claim now.
Congratulations! You have been randomly selected as one of only 10 users to participate in our exclusive beta testing for the new company portal.
We are distributing a limited number of complimentary coffee vouchers to staff this morning.

Urgency bias.definition: The tendency to prioritize immediate action or decision-making under perceived time pressure, often at the expense of accuracy or deliberation.
Urgency bias.phishing_context: Message provides a fake scenario that triggers a sense of pressure and suggests negative consequences if action is not taken immediately.
examples: Immediate action required: Your account will be locked in 30 minutes.
Unauthorized login attempt detected. Log in now to secure your funds.
Payment overdue. Click the link to settle your balance before service interruption.

Anchoring effect.definition: The tendency to rely too heavily on an initial value (anchor) when making judgments, insufficiently adjusting away from it.
Anchoring effect.phishing_context: Message highlights a prominent detail (e.g., a fabricated 'official' reference number) to anchor attention and divert scrutiny from other suspicious cues.
examples: Storage alert: You have used 1,989 MB of your 2,000 MB limit.
Invoice #84729 for $4,892 is overdue—confirm payment.
Your 2023 tax refund of $3,216 is pending—verify here.

Conformity bias.definition: The tendency to align one’s beliefs or behaviors with those of a group.
Conformity bias.phishing_context: Message implies that most of the victim’s social or professional group has already complied, prompting alignment with the perceived norm.
examples: 85% of your colleagues have already activated their new web portal. Join them today.
Due to recent activity, all account holders must comply with these new security steps.
Your team has recommended this shared document. Please log in to view the file.

Overconfidence.definition: The tendency for individuals to overestimate the accuracy of their knowledge, judgments, or abilities.
Overconfidence.phishing_context: Message targets an area where the victim feels knowledgeable, encouraging overconfidence and reduced scrutiny of subtle malicious cues.
examples: Commending your security awareness: Click here for a special security update for advanced users.
Based on your advanced technical profile, you have been selected to manually audit the new security protocols.
Your recent activity confirms your high compliance with company protocols. Please log in here to independently certify your session history and maintain your 'Expert-Level' security status.

Familiarity bias.definition: The tendency to prefer or assign greater validity to information, people, or options that are familiar.
Familiarity bias.phishing_context: Message involves the impersonation of a known trusted source or an acquaintance.
examples: Helping the university: Go [university mascot]! Log in to the new portal to show your support.
Hi [name], returning the favor from our last project. Check the attached details.
A friend has tagged you in a photo. Click here to view it on [social media website].
